Some people therefore prioritise higher production, whereas others favour environmental protection. In my view, productivity remains essential, but policy should pursue stable long-term yields rather than maximum short-term output.

Supporters of increased production emphasise population growth, price shocks and food security. Higher crop yields can reduce shortages and protect consumers from inflation. New seeds, irrigation and precision agriculture may help farms ratchet up output using fewer resources. What governments cannot ignore is that environmental reform loses support when households face unaffordable food. However, production based on deteriorating land is not durable. land degradation, soil erosion and fertiliser runoff reduce future productivity and damage water systems. Farmers may maintain output temporarily through greater input use, but this increases input dependence. Only when soil and water remain healthy can high yields be sustained across generations.

The best approach combines productivity with restoration. Governments should support crop diversification, cover crops and efficient irrigation while funding research into climate-resilient crops. Governments should taper off harmful subsidies, but farmers need transition payments, advice and insurance. Agricultural policy has often rewarded production volume, yet the wider environmental costs have remained outside the calculation. Farmers also need a credible transition pathway. New environmental requirements may involve equipment costs, temporary yield uncertainty and additional management. Multi-year contracts, extension services and insurance can protect farm income while allowing practices to change. This is especially important for smaller farms with limited access to credit.

Food policy should also reduce losses after harvest. Better storage and transport can increase effective supply without expanding farmland. Had earlier investment strengthened cold chains, more food might have reached consumers instead of spoiling. Governments should publish the nutritional and environmental results of farm support. Farmers should then receive practical advice and transition finance when policy asks them to change production methods.

In conclusion, agricultural policy should not choose between production and the environment. Its priority should be resilient productivity: sufficient food today without destroying the soil, water and ecosystems required for tomorrow’s harvests.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction treats adequate production and environmental durability as mutually dependent goals.

Causal explanation

The essay connects yields, affordability, soil condition, input dependence and long-term food security.

Developed contrast

The urgency of increasing supply is balanced against the danger of exhausting the resources agriculture needs.

Policy mechanism

Farm support, practical advice, transition finance and post-harvest investment turn a general position into a workable programme.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If governments protected soil earlier, fewer farms would now depend on expensive inputs. (Conditional inversion)

Had governments protected soil earlier, fewer farms would now depend on expensive inputs.

2. Farmers changed crops after drought had reduced yields. (Past perfect)

Farmers changed crops after drought had reduced yields.

3. Affordable healthy food matters most in food policy. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most in food policy is affordable access to healthy food.

4. Consumers will support reform only when prices remain manageable. (Negative inversion)

Only when prices remain manageable will consumers support reform.

5. Regenerative farming protects soil and reduces runoff. (Not only...but also)

Not only does regenerative farming protect soil, but it also reduces runoff.

6. The greenhouse was designed for efficiency, but it consumed excessive energy. (Participle clause)

Designed for efficiency, the greenhouse consumed excessive energy.

7. Although the subsidy supports income, it may still encourage harmful production. (Fronted concession)

Supportive of farm income though the subsidy may be, it may still encourage harmful production.

8. Governments should improve storage, protect soil and support healthy diets. (Controlled parallelism)

Governments should improve storage, protect soil and support healthy diets.

9. Food production has increased, but access remains unequal. (Present-perfect contrast)

Food production has increased, yet access remains unequal.

10. The retailer rejected the produce after the farmer had already harvested it. (Past perfect)

The retailer rejected the produce after the farmer had already harvested it.

11. The policy lacks coordination, so farmers face conflicting incentives. (Nominalisation)

The policy’s lack of coordination creates conflicting incentives for farmers.

12. If storage were more reliable, post-harvest losses would fall. (Conditional inversion)

Post-harvest losses would fall were storage more reliable.

13. Farmers opposed the reform because transition support was missing. (Cleft cause)

It was the absence of transition support that caused farmers to oppose the reform.

14. Policy should raise productivity and preserve soil fertility. (Balanced recommendation)

Policy should both raise productivity and preserve soil fertility.

15. The government introduced the scheme gradually, so farms had time to adapt. (Participle clause)

Introducing the scheme gradually, the government gave farms time to adapt.

16. Retailers changed contracts after regulators intervened. (Emphatic do)

Retailers did change contracts after regulators intervened.

17. No factor matters more than stable access to nutritious food. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any factor matter more than stable access to nutritious food.

18. The food system should be productive, resilient and fair. (Controlled parallelism)

The food system should sustain productivity, strengthen resilience and preserve fairness.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “The soil is getting worse.” using land degradation.

Land degradation is weakening the long-term productive capacity of agricultural land.

2. Upgrade: “Farmers use too many chemicals.” using pesticide load.

The current pesticide load creates avoidable risks for pollinators, water and farm workers.

3. Upgrade: “Different crops reduce risk.” using crop diversification.

Crop diversification can reduce exposure to weather, pests and market shocks.

4. Upgrade: “Farmers are paid too little.” using farm-gate prices.

Low farm-gate prices can undermine investment and long-term farm viability.

5. Upgrade: “Food becomes expensive during crises.” using food-price inflation.

Food-price inflation accelerates when harvest, energy and trade shocks occur together.

6. Upgrade: “A lot of food spoils before shops.” using post-harvest losses.

Post-harvest losses increase when storage, transport and refrigeration are inadequate.

7. Upgrade: “Healthy food is too expensive.” using healthy diets.

Healthy diets remain inaccessible when income growth fails to match food prices.

8. Upgrade: “Farmers need better information.” using precision agriculture.

Precision agriculture can improve decisions when data and tools remain accessible.

9. Upgrade: “The system relies on a few companies.” using market concentration.

Market concentration can weaken competition and farmer bargaining power.

10. Upgrade: “The government supports harmful production.” using agricultural subsidies.

Agricultural subsidies may unintentionally reward resource-intensive and polluting production.

11. Upgrade: “The farm is improving its soil.” using soil organic matter.

Increasing soil organic matter can improve fertility, structure and water retention.

12. Upgrade: “The farm uses several pest-control methods.” using integrated pest-management.

Integrated pest-management combines monitoring, crop practices and targeted chemical use.

13. Upgrade: “The country needs stronger food supplies.” using food-system resilience.

Food-system resilience depends on diverse production, trade, storage and social protection.

14. Upgrade: “The farm needs less water.” using water-use efficiency.

Improved water-use efficiency can protect production under increasing scarcity.

15. Upgrade: “The policy should connect farming and health.” using agriculture-nutrition policy alignment.

Effective agriculture-nutrition policy alignment is required across agriculture, nutrition, environment and social protection.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you try to avoid food waste?

Suggested phrasal verbs
throw awayratchet up
Model answer · 89 words

I try not to throw away edible food, mainly by planning meals and freezing leftovers. I also pare back impulse purchases when I know I will be busy. The problem is often poor organisation rather than a lack of concern. Clear date labels would help because people confuse safety dates with quality guidance. Reducing food waste saves money and lowers the pressure placed on farms, transport and disposal systems. I also check the fridge before shopping, since duplicate purchases are an impressively efficient route from supermarket shelf to bin.

PART 1 · 2

Do you buy local food?

Suggested phrasal verbs
taper offpare back
Model answer · 85 words

I buy local food when the quality and price are reasonable. Shorter supply chains can firm up regional businesses and make production more visible to consumers. However, local does not automatically mean environmentally superior, especially when heated greenhouses or inefficient transport are involved. Producers also need enough bargaining power so retailers do not pass through every risk to them while keeping most of the final price. Seasonal availability matters too, because local farms cannot provide every crop throughout the year without additional energy or storage.

PART 1 · 3

Would you like to grow vegetables?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pare backbuild back
Model answer · 87 words

I would like to grow a few vegetables and herbs because it would make seasonal production more tangible. Compost and cover plants could build back soil quality over time. I might also change over to edible varieties, including species that provide food or pollinator habitat. I would not expect a small garden to provide complete self-sufficiency, but it could reduce waste and help me understand why weather, pests and timing matter. Even one successful crop would make the connection between soil, water and food far less theoretical.

PART 1 · 4

Do food prices affect what you buy?

Suggested phrasal verbs
drive upbuild back
Model answer · 87 words

Food prices strongly affect my choices. When inflation can drive up the cost of meat, fruit or dairy, I pare back expensive items and choose simpler meals. This does not always mean a less healthy diet, but low-income households have fewer alternatives. food-price inflation becomes especially harmful when wages and benefits do not rise at the same speed. Affordability therefore matters as much as the physical availability of food. People with limited budgets may respond by buying cheaper calories rather than the foods they would ideally choose.

PART 1 · 5

Do you read food labels?

Suggested phrasal verbs
break downflow through to
Model answer · 82 words

I read labels for ingredients, nutrition and origin, although the information is sometimes difficult to compare. A useful label should break down the most important facts without turning the package into a technical report. Production standards and transport can flow through to environmental impact, but one simple score may hide trade-offs. Transparent communication is more helpful than vague terms such as “natural” or “farm fresh,” which often function mainly as advertising. Independent standards would make environmental claims easier to trust and compare.

PART 1 · 6

Would you work on a farm?

Suggested phrasal verbs
run shortflow through to
Model answer · 89 words

I would try seasonal farm work for a short period, but I would not underestimate how physically demanding it is. Farms increasingly put in place machinery and digital tools, yet workers still face heat, long hours and uncertain contracts. During harvest, employers may run short of labour at exactly the wrong moment. Better pay, accommodation and safety standards are necessary if agriculture expects people to treat farm work as skilled employment rather than disposable labour. Mechanisation should improve conditions rather than simply increase the pace expected from fewer workers.

PART 1 · 7

Do you prefer supermarkets or farmers’ markets?

Suggested phrasal verbs
drive uprun short
Model answer · 83 words

Supermarkets are more convenient and usually offer consistent prices, while farmers’ markets provide direct contact with producers. Buying directly can firm up farm income because fewer intermediaries take a share. On the other hand, small producers may still need to pass through high transport and production costs. I use both, depending on price and availability. A resilient food system probably needs diverse retail channels rather than one ideal model. Digital ordering can complement both models, although delivery costs and packaging also deserve attention.

PART 1 · 8

Would you eat food grown in a greenhouse?

Suggested phrasal verbs
run shortfirm up
Model answer · 85 words

I would eat greenhouse-grown food without hesitation. In practice, greenhouse farming can stabilise vegetable production and ratchet up supply in colder regions. Efficient lighting, recycled water and renewable energy may push down environmental costs, although heated structures can use substantial energy. The method should be judged by the entire system rather than the word “greenhouse.” It can improve local availability, but it is not automatically sustainable or affordable. Transparent energy data would help consumers distinguish efficient protected farming from systems dependent on high fossil-fuel use.

PART 1 · 9

Do you think organic food is worth the price?

Suggested phrasal verbs
firm upput in place
Model answer · 82 words

Organic standards can pare back some synthetic pesticides and encourage practices that build back soil health. However, the price is often high, and the label does not guarantee low emissions, fair labour or local production. I buy organic selectively rather than treating it as the only responsible choice. Stronger general farming standards may have a wider effect than relying on wealthier consumers to purchase a premium category. Farmers also need transition support, since environmental improvements may involve temporary risk and unfamiliar management.

PART 1 · 10

Have you noticed changing food seasons?

Suggested phrasal verbs
run shortdrive up
Model answer · 84 words

Some foods are available all year, so natural seasons are easy to forget. However, poor harvests can make shops run short of particular vegetables and drive up prices. Climate change may make these disruptions more frequent. I try to notice which products are naturally abundant at different times because seasonal food is often cheaper and better. Still, international trade remains useful when local production is temporarily weak. Better storage and crop diversity can reduce the severity of seasonal shortages without pretending trade is unnecessary.

PART 1 · 11

Would you eat less meat for environmental reasons?

Suggested phrasal verbs
change over topush down
Model answer · 84 words

I could pare back meat consumption without removing it completely. Replacing several meals with beans, grains or vegetables can reduce land pressure and household costs. I would rather change over to a lower-meat diet gradually than follow a rigid diet that becomes difficult to maintain. Policy should also improve the quality of plant-based options and support livestock farmers during change. Individual choices matter, but pricing, availability and culture shape those choices. Public institutions can make lower-impact meals normal through schools, hospitals and workplace catering.

PART 1 · 12

Do you trust new farming technology?

Suggested phrasal verbs
push downpass through
Model answer · 80 words

I trust technology when it is tested transparently and solves a clear problem. Sensors, weather forecasts and precision agriculture can reduce wasted water or fertiliser. However, companies should not put in place systems that make farmers dependent on expensive subscriptions or opaque algorithms. Before governments ratchet up support, independent trials should examine cost, data ownership and performance on small farms as well as large operations. Farmers should retain control over information collected from their land, machinery and workers. That matters.

PART 1 · 13

Is cooking an important skill?

Suggested phrasal verbs
throw awaypass through
Model answer · 86 words

Cooking is important because it helps people use affordable ingredients and pare back dependence on highly processed convenience food. It also makes it easier to use leftovers rather than throw away food that does not fit one planned recipe. However, cooking requires time, equipment and confidence. Schools can teach basic skills, but governments should not use personal responsibility to ignore low wages, long working hours or poor access to healthy food. Community kitchens and affordable ingredients can make this skill accessible beyond households with abundant time.

PART 1 · 14

Do you care how farmers treat the soil?

Suggested phrasal verbs
break downthrow away
Model answer · 83 words

I care because soil determines whether farming remains productive during drought and heavy rain. Cover crops and compost can build back soil organic matter, while microorganisms break down residues and release nutrients. Consumers rarely see these processes, so soil health receives less attention than packaging or animal welfare. Public policy should reward measurable improvement because the benefits continue beyond one harvest and extend to water, biodiversity and climate resilience. Protecting soil is cheaper than trying to rebuild fertility after erosion has become severe.

PART 1 · 15

Would you support limits on pesticides?

Suggested phrasal verbs
sell offratchet up
Model answer · 83 words

I would support limits based on toxicity and ecological risk rather than a simple ban on every product. Governments should taper off the most harmful chemicals and help farmers push down the overall pesticide load through integrated pest-management. Farmers still need reliable ways to protect crops, so research, monitoring and advice are essential. A rule without practical alternatives may reduce yields or encourage illegal use instead of producing safer agriculture. Monitoring should measure ecological impact, not merely the weight of chemical products sold.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

How can countries make food systems more resilient?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ratchet uptaper off
Model answer · 120 words

Countries can firm up food-system resilience by diversifying crops, suppliers and transport routes. They should ratchet up storage, climate information and research into climate-resilient crops. Resilience also depends on household purchasing power because an adequate national food supply does not guarantee access. Strategic reserves may help during short shocks, but they should not replace functioning trade. Governments need reliable data on stocks, harvests and prices, plus social protection that activates quickly. The strongest system combines productive farms, healthy soils, flexible supply chains and institutions capable of responding before shortages become humanitarian emergencies. Regional processing and storage can also prevent one damaged port or factory from interrupting the entire chain. Resilience is therefore created through redundancy rather than maximum short-term efficiency.

PART 3 · 2

Is regenerative agriculture a realistic solution?

Suggested phrasal verbs
taper offpare back
Model answer · 128 words

Overall, regenerative agriculture is promising because practices such as cover crops, rotations and reduced tillage can build back soil organic matter and improve water retention. However, the term lacks one universal definition, and results vary by soil, climate and crop. Governments should not ratchet up branded programmes before monitoring establishes what works. Transition periods can reduce income or require new equipment, so farmers need advice and financial support. Regenerative methods are best treated as a direction rather than a miracle label. Their credibility depends on farm-level performance indicators, long-term trials and honest comparison with other forms of sustainable farming. Farmers should be rewarded for verified outcomes rather than for adopting a fashionable vocabulary. Research must also distinguish improvements caused by the practice from unusually favourable weather or prices.

PART 3 · 3

Should governments subsidise farming?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pare backbuild back
Model answer · 119 words

Agriculture deserves support because farms provide food security, rural employment and environmental services. However, governments should taper off subsidies that encourage excessive fertiliser, water use or production in unsuitable areas. Support should instead firm up soil restoration, risk management and the transition to more resilient practices. Payments need clear public outcomes rather than automatic links to land ownership. Small farmers often lack the administrative capacity to access complex schemes, while large landowners capture most funding. A fair system would combine income stability with environmental conditions, transparent reporting and technical advice. Public support can include insurance and credit during transition, because environmental reform will lose legitimacy if farmers experience it only as additional paperwork and income risk. That balance matters.

PART 3 · 4

How does climate change threaten agriculture?

Suggested phrasal verbs
run shortdrive up
Model answer · 128 words

Heat, drought, floods and changing pest patterns affect crops, livestock and farm labour. Rainfed regions may run short of water, while repeated harvest losses drive up food prices. Heat can reduce worker safety and create heat-stressed livestock. Climate effects also interact with soil degradation, conflict and high input costs. Adaptation requires crop diversification, efficient irrigation, warnings and insurance, but there are limits if warming continues. Agricultural policy must therefore combine climate adaptation with emissions reduction rather than assuming farmers can adjust indefinitely to worsening conditions. Labour rules must protect outdoor workers during dangerous heat, since food production cannot be called resilient when the people harvesting it are exposed to preventable illness. Public crop insurance should reward risk reduction rather than repeatedly compensating production in locations becoming less viable.

PART 3 · 5

Can technology solve global hunger?

Suggested phrasal verbs
break downflow through to
Model answer · 119 words

Technology can improve production, storage and market information, but hunger is not caused only by insufficient output. Governments can put in place weather services, improved seeds and cold chains, while efficiency may push down some costs. Yet people remain hungry when incomes collapse, conflict blocks access or healthy diets are unaffordable. Technology may also widen inequality if only large farms can purchase it. The solution requires infrastructure, peace, social protection and fair markets alongside innovation. Technology is an important tool, but treating hunger as an engineering problem hides its political and economic causes. Public research remains important because commercial firms naturally focus on products they can sell. Open data and farmer training determine whether innovation spreads beyond wealthy producers.

PART 3 · 6

Should farms reduce pesticide use?

Suggested phrasal verbs
flow through todrive up
Model answer · 128 words

Farms should pare back pesticide use where non-chemical controls can protect yields effectively. Governments should taper off substances with unacceptable risks to health, pollinators or water. An integrated pest-management strategy combines crop rotation, monitoring, resistant varieties and natural predators, reducing routine spraying. However, abrupt restrictions without advice or alternatives can create crop losses and farmer resistance. Targets should consider toxicity and frequency rather than volume alone. Public extension services are necessary because farmers should not bear the transition risk while society receives the environmental benefit. Retailers and food processors should share responsibility by accepting greater crop variation and contracting fairly. Otherwise, environmental standards become another cost imposed only at farm level. Extension services can demonstrate non-chemical methods, since farmers are unlikely to risk a harvest on generic advice.

PART 3 · 7

Why is soil degradation a national problem?

Suggested phrasal verbs
break downdrive up
Model answer · 124 words

Soil organisms break down organic matter, store water and support crop nutrition. When land degradation damages structure and fertility, yields become more vulnerable to drought and heavy rain. These effects flow through to food prices, flood risk and rural income, making soil a national infrastructure issue rather than a private farm concern. Degradation may remain hidden for years because fertiliser temporarily maintains output. Governments should monitor soil condition, reward restoration and prevent erosion. Rebuilding soil takes much longer than exhausting it, so delay creates serious long-term costs. Soil policy also protects water systems because stronger structure reduces runoff and improves infiltration. These wider benefits justify public investment and long-term monitoring. Soil protection also reduces the need for expensive emergency support after floods or droughts.

PART 3 · 8

How can food waste be reduced?

Suggested phrasal verbs
throw awayrun short
Model answer · 128 words

Households, retailers and restaurants throw away food for different reasons, so one policy will not solve the problem. Cities can put in place measurement, separate collection and public campaigns. Retailers should improve forecasting, relax cosmetic standards and donate safe surplus food. Clear date labels help consumers distinguish quality from safety. Earlier in the chain, storage and cold-chain capacity reduce post-harvest losses. Prevention should come before recycling because the land, water and energy used to produce wasted food cannot be recovered simply by composting it. Food businesses should publish waste data, because voluntary claims are difficult to evaluate. Procurement rules can reward suppliers that prevent loss rather than merely diverting waste after it occurs. Municipal contracts for schools and hospitals can create demand for surplus redistribution and imperfect produce.

PART 3 · 9

Should people be encouraged to eat less meat?

Suggested phrasal verbs
firm upput in place
Model answer · 126 words

In high-consuming countries, encouraging people to pare back meat can reduce land demand and emissions. Some consumers can change over to meals based on legumes, grains and vegetables without sacrificing nutrition. However, policy should distinguish intensive production from pastoral systems and recognise that livestock supports many rural livelihoods. Taxes or campaigns may be regressive if healthy alternatives remain expensive. Governments should improve public catering, information and plant-based availability while supporting farmers who diversify. Gradual dietary change is more credible than moralising or pretending one diet suits every region. Guidance should remain culturally sensitive and precise. The goal is lower environmental pressure, not a symbolic competition over personal virtue. Livestock policy should also address methane, feed sourcing and animal welfare rather than focusing only on consumer behaviour.

PART 3 · 10

Do vertical farms improve food security?

Suggested phrasal verbs
put in placechange over to
Model answer · 120 words

Controlled-environment farms can ratchet up production of leafy vegetables near cities and use water efficiently. Technology may push down transport losses and provide consistent output. However, energy costs are substantial, and these systems cannot currently replace staple grains, pulses or most field crops. Their contribution to food security is therefore limited but useful, especially for fresh produce in harsh climates. Projects should be evaluated against efficient greenhouses and conventional farms rather than promotional claims. The best role may be specialised urban production, research and seedling supply. Greenhouses and vertical systems may also provide seedlings, research environments and rapid production after disasters. Their energy source is decisive, because highly controlled production can exchange land and water savings for substantial electricity demand.

PART 3 · 11

How should small farmers be supported?

Suggested phrasal verbs
change over topush down
Model answer · 126 words

Governments should firm up smallholder farmers through affordable credit, insurance, storage and fair market access. They can put in place extension services that combine local knowledge with climate forecasts and soil advice. Support should not force farmers into expensive input packages or contracts they do not understand. Farmer cooperatives can improve bargaining power and shared equipment. Women and tenant farmers need secure access to land and finance. The goal is to give rural households genuine productive choices. Social protection is necessary when weather destroys harvests, but it should complement investment in irrigation, roads and market systems rather than becoming a permanent substitute for productive capacity. Mobile banking and digital marketplaces may help, provided data charges and platform fees do not create a new form of dependency.

PART 3 · 12

Can global trade strengthen food security?

Suggested phrasal verbs
push downpass through
Model answer · 122 words

Trade can firm up food security when local harvests fail because countries obtain food from regions with better conditions. It also allows specialised production and lowers some prices. However, concentrated suppliers, export bans and transport disruption create vulnerability. Traders and retailers may pass through sudden costs rapidly to consumers while farmers receive little benefit. Countries should maintain diverse import sources, transparent reserves and domestic production capacity for essential foods. Complete self-sufficiency is inefficient, but complete dependence on a few global routes is equally risky. International rules should discourage sudden export restrictions that intensify panic. Cooperation is easiest before a crisis, when governments are not already competing for scarce supplies. Regional agreements can coordinate stocks and transport corridors before shortages encourage unilateral restrictions.

PART 3 · 13

Should food retailers be regulated more strictly?

Suggested phrasal verbs
throw awaypass through
Model answer · 123 words

Large food retailers influence prices, farm contracts, product standards and waste. Regulation should prevent unfair purchasing practices that pass through excessive risk to farmers. Retailers should report how much food they throw away, improve donation systems and reduce cosmetic rejection of produce. However, rules must avoid creating paperwork that simply shifts waste back to suppliers. Competition policy also matters where market concentration gives a few chains strong bargaining power. Regulation should focus on transparent contracts, waste prevention and consumer access to affordable healthy food. Retailers should also disclose payment periods and contract changes, because delayed payment can damage small farms even when supermarket shelves remain full. Stronger rules can also stop suppliers being charged for promotions or rejected at short notice without compensation.

PART 3 · 14

What makes an agricultural transition fair?

Suggested phrasal verbs
throw awaysell off
Model answer · 128 words

A fair transition gives farmers time, finance and practical alternatives. Farms asked to change over to lower-input systems may face yield risk, equipment costs and uncertain markets. Governments should build back advisory services, insurance and stable payments for verified public benefits. Farm workers also need training and labour protection. Policies should recognise differences among regions and farm sizes instead of imposing one schedule. A just transition succeeds when environmental improvement strengthens rural livelihoods rather than transferring the entire cost to producers with weak bargaining power. Worker participation and farmer organisations can identify which reforms are technically realistic. A transition designed entirely by ministries and consultants is unlikely to survive difficult seasons. Long-term contracts can give farmers enough certainty to invest in new systems and wait for soil improvements.

PART 3 · 15

What would a sustainable food system look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
sell offratchet up
Model answer · 119 words

A sustainable food system would provide affordable healthy diets, decent rural employment and stable production without degrading soil, water or biodiversity. It would pare back unnecessary inputs and waste while using technology selectively. Diverse farms, storage and trade would firm up resilience to climate and political shocks. Public policy would align agricultural subsidies, health goals and environmental standards. Consumers would receive clear information, but responsibility would not be reduced to shopping choices. Sustainability is achieved when the institutions shaping production and access support multi-season outcomes rather than maximising short-term volume alone. Animal welfare, land rights and nutrition must also be included. A food system that lowers emissions while exploiting workers or making healthy diets unaffordable is not genuinely sustainable.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Governments should redirect agricultural subsidies from production volume towards environmental outcomes. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
farm incomepollinator habitatcover cropscrop rotationreduced tillagelong-term public valueagrifood systemsfood-system resilienceland degradation
Model essay · 337 words

Many governments support agriculture through payments connected to land area or production. Critics argue that these schemes encourage intensive farming and should be redirected towards environmental outcomes. I largely agree, although income stability and food security must remain part of the system.

Traditional subsidies can protect farm income and strategic production. However, payments based mainly on volume may reward excessive fertiliser, water use or cultivation of unsuitable land. What public money should purchase is a clear nutrition dividend, not merely more output regardless of its wider cost. Governments should therefore support soil restoration, pollinator habitat, water protection and lower pesticide risk. Payments could help farmers adopt cover crops, crop rotation or reduced tillage. These practices may create long-term public value that one farmer cannot fully capture through market prices.

The main risk is transition. Environmental schemes can involve complex applications, delayed payment and uncertain results. Small farms may lose support while large businesses employ consultants to satisfy the rules. Were payments based on verified but flexible outcomes, farmers could adapt methods to local conditions. Reform should be gradual and accompanied by advice, insurance and stable multi-year contracts. Governments must also protect production capacity for essential foods. Redirecting support does not mean abandoning farmers to volatile markets.

Monitoring is crucial. Soil, water and habitat indicators should be transparent, while paperwork should remain proportionate. Payments should reward genuine improvement rather than attractive plans that are never implemented. A further issue is regional fairness. Payments that reward only visible biodiversity projects may neglect productive areas where soil and water improvement would have greater food-security value. National programmes should therefore combine common standards with local priorities. Farmer organisations should participate in designing indicators. This would improve practicality and reduce the suspicion that reform is being imposed by officials who will never manage a difficult harvest.

In conclusion: agricultural subsidies should move away from production volume and towards environmental outcomes. The reform will succeed only if it provides predictable income, practical support and credible measurement rather than transferring new risks to farmers.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe technological farming is essential for future food security, while others prefer traditional and regenerative methods. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
precision agricultureinput dependencemarket concentrationregenerative agricultureagrifood systemsfood-system resilienceland degradationsoil organic mattersoil fertility
Model essay · 326 words

Future agriculture will need technology, but it will also depend on ecological knowledge and traditional management. Some people favour sensors, automation and controlled environments, whereas others support regenerative or low-input farming. In my view, these approaches should be combined rather than treated as ideological rivals.

Technology can improve accuracy. precision agriculture allows farms to apply water, fertiliser and pesticides where needed. Forecasts and improved seeds can help farmers respond to drought and disease. Controlled systems may ratchet up vegetable production with efficient water use. What technology offers is greater control over timing, measurement and input use. However, expensive machinery and proprietary software may increase input dependence and market concentration. A farm can become efficient yet vulnerable to subscription costs, equipment failure or one supplier. Technology also cannot replace healthy soil. Together, regenerative agriculture, rotations and agroforestry build back ecological resilience that remains valuable when machines fail.

Traditional knowledge is equally important because farming is local. Farmers understand soil, seasons and animal behaviour in ways that a general model may miss. Nevertheless, tradition should not protect harmful practices from evidence. The best system uses technology to support ecological farming. Sensors can measure soil moisture, while rotations and cover crops improve the soil being measured. Not only should innovation raise yields, but it should also reduce risk and environmental damage.

Public research and open standards can make tools accessible to smaller farms. Independent trials should compare multi-season outcomes, not only one season’s output. Education is also important. Agricultural colleges should teach soil biology, machinery, data analysis and farm economics together. Separating “traditional” and “technological” knowledge creates graduates who understand one tool but not the whole system. Farmers must retain ownership or meaningful control of data generated by equipment. Otherwise, digital efficiency may be purchased through long-term dependence on a small number of platforms.

In conclusion: technology is essential but insufficient. Future food security depends on combining modern measurement with farming practices that preserve soil, biodiversity and farmer independence.

TASK 2 · 3

Countries are investing in greenhouse and vertical farming near cities. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
drip irrigationwater-use efficiencymarket concentrationagrifood systemsfood-system resilienceland degradationsoil organic mattersoil fertilitysoil structure
Model essay · 307 words

Greenhouses and vertical farms promise fresh production close to consumers. Their benefits are significant, but I believe they outweigh the disadvantages only for suitable crops and energy systems.

The main advantage is control. Protected farms can manage temperature, water and pests, producing vegetables throughout the year. drip irrigation and recirculation improve water-use efficiency, while local production may reduce some transport losses. What these systems provide is stability when outdoor conditions are unreliable. They can also use limited urban land and supply restaurants or supermarkets quickly. During disruption, nearby production may firm up access to perishable food.

The disadvantages are energy and cost. Lighting, heating and cooling can make production expensive and carbon-intensive. Vertical farms are mainly suitable for leafy vegetables rather than grains, pulses or livestock feed. High capital costs may also favour large investors and increase market concentration. Only when clean energy and realistic markets are available do these systems become broadly defensible. Governments should support research and transparent life-cycle assessment rather than subsidise impressive buildings without demand. Greenhouses should be compared with efficient field production in appropriate climates. Waste heat, renewable electricity and recycled water can improve performance.

These systems may also supply seedlings, conduct research and stabilise specialised crops after disasters. Their role is valuable but limited. Labour conditions deserve attention because controlled farms may replace seasonal outdoor work with technical indoor jobs. This can improve safety and stability, but workers need training and protection from repetitive monitoring. Public procurement can provide an early market for efficient local production, although contracts should remain competitive and should not guarantee demand regardless of cost or quality.

In conclusion: urban controlled agriculture can improve water efficiency and fresh-food reliability. Its advantages exceed the disadvantages when technology is matched to appropriate crops and low-carbon energy, not when it is presented as a replacement for farming as a whole.

TASK 2 · 4

Large quantities of food are lost or wasted before they are eaten. What problems does this cause, and what solutions should governments and businesses introduce?

Optional collocation bank
post-harvest lossesthrow awaycold-chain capacityagrifood systemsfood-system resilienceland degradationsoil organic mattersoil fertilitysoil structure
Model essay · 309 words

Food is lost on farms and during storage, while retailers, restaurants and households waste edible products. This damages food security, household budgets and the environment.

Before retail, weak roads, pests and inadequate refrigeration create post-harvest losses. Farmers receive no income from food that never reaches the market, while consumers face lower supply and higher prices. Later, businesses and households throw away food because of inaccurate forecasting, cosmetic standards or confusing date labels. What is wasted is not only the product but also the land, water, energy and labour used to produce it. Governments should invest in roads, storage and cold-chain capacity. Small farmers need shared facilities and affordable finance rather than expensive systems designed only for large exporters. Digital market information can match supply with buyers earlier.

Retailers should publish comparable waste data, improve ordering and accept greater variation in appearance. Safe surplus food can be donated, although prevention is better than redistribution. Clear date labels should distinguish safety from quality. Households also need practical information about meal planning, freezing and portion size. Not only should cities collect food waste separately, but they should also measure whether prevention is reducing the amount generated.

Contracts matter because retailers may shift rejected produce back to suppliers. Regulation should prevent this hidden transfer of waste. Food businesses should also reconsider promotions that encourage excessive purchasing. Smaller package sizes and flexible portions may reduce household waste, while restaurants can offer containers or variable serving sizes. Waste policy must avoid a moral hierarchy in which consumers are blamed while retailers continue rejecting crops for appearance or cancelling orders at short notice. Public procurement standards can also reward suppliers that prevent waste before it occurs.

In conclusion: food loss and waste raise prices, waste resources and reduce farmer income. Better infrastructure, transparent retail practices and clearer consumer guidance can address different stages of the problem.

TASK 2 · 5

Why do food prices rise sharply during climate and geopolitical crises? How can governments protect consumers without damaging farmers?

Optional collocation bank
crop failuredrive upagrifood systemsfood-system resilienceland degradationsoil organic mattersoil fertilitysoil structuresoil microbiome
Model essay · 307 words

Food prices often rise rapidly during droughts, conflict or energy crises because several pressures occur simultaneously. Harvest losses reduce supply, while transport, fertiliser and fuel become more expensive.

Climate events can cause crop failure across major producing regions. Export restrictions then amplify fear, and traders may drive up prices as buyers compete for limited stocks. Energy costs also flow through to fertiliser, processing and transport. When supply chains are concentrated, one blocked route or producer creates a large shock. Governments must protect consumers, particularly low-income households, but broad price controls can damage farmers. If official prices fall below production costs, farms reduce output or sell through informal markets. What households need is purchasing power, not a policy that makes production financially impossible.

Targeted cash transfers, food vouchers and school meals can protect access. Strategic reserves may stabilise short disruptions, provided releases follow transparent rules. Governments should also diversify imports and firm up domestic storage. Farmers need insurance, affordable credit and temporary support for high input costs. Had earlier policy invested in resilient crops and irrigation, some harvest losses might have been smaller. Support should reward adaptation rather than preserve production in locations becoming unviable.

International cooperation can reduce sudden export bans and keep transport corridors open. Market data should be published to discourage panic and manipulation. Longer-term reform should reduce vulnerability before the next crisis. Crop diversification, soil restoration and regional processing can make production less sensitive to one weather event. Competition policy can also reduce dependence on a few traders or retailers. Consumer support should be automatic and temporary, expanding when prices rise and declining as conditions stabilise. This protects access without permanently distorting production incentives.

In conclusion: crisis prices reflect interacting production, energy and trade shocks. Targeted consumer support, resilient farming and transparent reserves can protect access without forcing farmers to absorb the entire cost.
